definition: Linear regression predicts a numeric value by drawing the straight line that best fits the training points, minimizing the squared distance between the line and the data.
question: A model predicts house PRICE from floor area by fitting a straight line. What kind of problem is this?
options: Regression, because the output is a continuous number
Classification, because houses fall into categories
Clustering, because similar houses group together
vocab: Residual: the vertical gap between a data point and the fitted line. Training tunes the line to keep residuals small.

tutor.name: Sage Linna
pages: Welcome to Slope Meadow, traveler. See those haystacks? Bigger fields grow bigger stacks. If I plot field size against stack height, the dots form a rough upward trend. Linear regression is the art of drawing ONE straight line through those dots so it follows the trend as closely as possible.

The line is defined by a slope and an intercept. To judge a candidate line, measure each dot's miss, square it so misses cannot cancel out, and add them up. The best line is the one with the smallest total squared miss. Once you have it, predicting is easy: walk along the line to your input and read off the answer.

mini.name: Slopeling
mini.question: In the line y = 3x + 2, what does the 3 represent?
options: How much y changes when x increases by 1
The value of y when x is 0
The number of data points needed

definition: Logistic regression is a classifier: it passes a weighted sum of the inputs through the S-shaped sigmoid function to output a probability between 0 and 1, then applies a threshold to pick a class.
question: Logistic regression outputs 0.85 for an email. What does that number mean?
options: An estimated 85 percent probability the email belongs to the positive class
The email contains 85 percent spam words
The model is 85 percent finished training
vocab: Decision threshold: the probability cutoff (often 0.5) above which the model predicts the positive class.

tutor.name: Oracle Logit
pages: This river crossing asks a yes-or-no question: will the traveler make it across? A straight line can score travelers, but scores run from minus infinity to plus infinity, and I want a PROBABILITY. So I feed the score into the sigmoid, a gentle S-shaped curve that squashes any number into the range 0 to 1.

A score of 0 lands exactly on 0.5, huge scores approach 1, and very negative scores approach 0. To decide, I pick a threshold: above 0.5, predict YES. Despite the name, logistic regression is used for classification. It is often the first classifier you should try, and its weights tell you which features push the odds up or down.

mini.name: Sigmoid Slime
mini.question: What range of values can the sigmoid function output?
options: Strictly between 0 and 1
Any number from minus 1 to 1
Only exactly 0 or exactly 1

definition: K-nearest neighbors classifies a new point by finding the k closest training points and letting them vote: the new point gets the majority label among its neighbors.
question: With k = 5, a new point's nearest neighbors are 3 wolves and 2 sheep. What does KNN predict?
options: Wolf, because wolves hold the majority of the 5 votes
Sheep, because sheep appeared most recently
Nothing, because the vote must be unanimous
vocab: Lazy learning: KNN does no work at training time. It memorizes the data and does all the effort at prediction time.

tutor.name: Elder Nearby
pages: In Neighbor Hollow we have a saying: you are what your neighbors are. To classify a stranger, KNN measures the distance from the stranger to every villager it has ever met, takes the k closest ones, and holds a vote. Majority label wins. No line fitting, no training. Just distance and democracy.

The choice of k matters. k = 1 trusts a single neighbor and gets fooled by noise. A huge k drowns local detail in the crowd. Odd values of k avoid tie votes. One more warning: distance is meaningless if one feature is measured in gold coins and another in millimeters, so SCALE your features before letting them vote.

mini.name: Kloseby
mini.question: Why should features be scaled before using KNN?
options: So no single large-valued feature dominates the distance calculation
Because KNN can only handle values between 0 and 1
To make the training phase run faster

definition: A decision tree classifies by asking a sequence of simple yes-or-no questions about the features, splitting the data at each branch, until it reaches a leaf that gives the prediction.
question: How does a decision tree decide which feature question to ask at a split?
options: It picks the split that best separates the classes, for example by reducing impurity the most
It always asks about the features in alphabetical order
It chooses a feature at random every time
vocab: Leaf node: the end of a branch. Whatever training examples land there decide the prediction for that path.

tutor.name: Keeper Root
pages: Every path through this glade is a chain of questions. Is the mushroom cap red? Go left. Wider than your palm? Go right. A decision tree learns which questions to ask, and in what order, so that by the time you reach a leaf, almost everything gathered there shares one label.

At each split the tree greedily picks the question that unmixes the classes the most, measured by impurity scores such as Gini or entropy. Trees are wonderfully easy to read, and they do not care about feature scaling. But grow one too deep and it memorizes every twig of the training data. Remember that for the marsh ahead.

mini.name: Twigling
mini.question: What is a key advantage of decision trees over many other models?
options: Their decisions are easy for humans to read and interpret
They can never overfit the training data
They require enormous amounts of data to work at all

definition: Overfitting is when a model memorizes noise and quirks of its training data instead of the real pattern, so it scores well on data it has seen but poorly on new data. A held-out test set reveals it.
question: A model scores 99 percent on its training data but 62 percent on fresh test data. What happened?
options: It overfit: it memorized the training data instead of learning the general pattern
It underfit: the model is too simple for the problem
Nothing is wrong: training accuracy is the score that matters
vocab: Generalization: how well a model performs on data it was never trained on. It is the only score that truly matters.

tutor.name: Warden Split
pages: Careful in Mirror Marsh. The water shows you a perfect reflection of yourself, and models can drown in their own reflection too. A model that studies its training data too hard starts memorizing noise: this exact frog at this exact spot croaked at dawn. Flawless on the past, useless on tomorrow. That is overfitting.

The cure starts with honesty: before training, lock part of your data in a chest and never let the model see it. Train on the rest, then test on the chest. A large gap between training score and test score is the marsh whispering that you memorized. Simpler models, more data, and regularization all help close the gap.

mini.name: Memorizer Mite
mini.question: Why must the test set stay hidden during training?
options: So it can give an honest estimate of performance on unseen data
Because test data is stored in a different file format
To make training finish more quickly

definition: Naive Bayes classifies using Bayes' rule: it combines the prior probability of each class with the likelihood of the observed features, naively assuming the features are independent of each other given the class.
question: What is the 'naive' assumption in Naive Bayes?
options: That every feature is independent of the others once you know the class
That all classes are equally common in the data
That the data contains no errors of any kind
vocab: Prior: what you believe about class probabilities before seeing any features, usually taken from class frequencies in the training data.

tutor.name: Friar Bayes
pages: Welcome to the bazaar, where every judgment is an update. Suppose one stall in ten sells counterfeits: that is my PRIOR. Then I spot a clue, say a suspiciously low price. Bayes' rule tells me exactly how much that clue should shift my belief, by weighing how likely such a price is at a counterfeit stall versus an honest one.

With many clues the honest math gets tangled, so Naive Bayes makes a bold simplification: treat every clue as independent given the class, and just multiply their evidence together. It is rarely strictly true, yet the classifier works remarkably well, trains in a blink, and once ruled the realm of spam filtering.

mini.name: Spam Imp
mini.question: Naive Bayes multiplies together evidence from each feature. This works because it assumes what?
options: Feature independence given the class
That features are all measured in the same units
That there are exactly two classes

definition: A support vector machine separates two classes with the boundary that leaves the widest possible margin between them. The few training points touching the margin are the support vectors that define it.
question: Which training points actually determine an SVM's decision boundary?
options: The support vectors: the points lying closest to the boundary
All points, each weighted exactly equally
Only points the model misclassified during training
vocab: Kernel trick: a shortcut that lets an SVM act as if the data were mapped into a higher-dimensional space, bending straight boundaries into curves without paying for the mapping.

tutor.name: Knight Marginal
pages: Behold the keep's wall. Many walls could separate the two armies camped on this field, but only one stands with the widest clear ground on both sides. That is the SVM way: among all boundaries that split the classes, choose the one with the maximum margin. Wide margins forgive small errors in where new soldiers stand.

Curiously, most soldiers do not matter to the wall at all. Only the few standing right at the edge of the margin hold it in place. We call them support vectors. And when no straight wall can split the armies, the kernel trick lifts the battlefield into a higher dimension where a straight wall exists, then casts its shadow back down as a curve.

mini.name: Margin Wisp
mini.question: Why does an SVM prefer the widest margin?
options: A wide margin tends to generalize better to new points
It makes the model train faster
Wide margins use less memory

definition: A random forest trains many decision trees, each on a random bootstrap sample of the data and random subsets of features, then averages their votes. The ensemble is far more stable than any single tree.
question: Why does a random forest usually beat a single decision tree?
options: Averaging many diverse trees cancels out their individual overfitting errors
Each tree in the forest is allowed to grow much deeper
The forest trains on more total data than one tree could
vocab: Bootstrap sample: a training set drawn from the original data by sampling WITH replacement, so some rows repeat and others are left out.

tutor.name: Grove Mother Ballot
pages: One tree is clever but jumpy: change a few training examples and it may grow into a completely different shape. So we plant a forest. Each tree trains on its own bootstrap sample, drawn from the data with replacement, and at every split it may only consider a random handful of features. Each tree ends up opinionated and slightly wrong in its own way.

Then we vote. Errors that point in different directions cancel; the shared signal survives. That is bagging: bootstrap aggregating. The randomness is not a bug, it is the whole point, because averaging only helps when the voices differ. Random forests are sturdy, hard to overfit, and a superb default for tabular data.

mini.name: Ballot Sprite
mini.question: In bagging, how is each model's training set created?
options: By sampling the original data randomly with replacement
By splitting the data into equal non-overlapping chunks
By sorting the data and giving each model one slice

definition: Gradient boosting builds an ensemble one small tree at a time, where each new tree is trained to correct the errors that the ensemble so far still makes. Predictions are the sum of all the trees.
question: In gradient boosting, what is each new tree trained to do?
options: Fix the remaining errors of the ensemble built so far
Classify the same data independently of the other trees
Copy the strongest tree found in earlier rounds
vocab: Learning rate: a small factor that shrinks each new tree's contribution, trading more rounds for better generalization.

tutor.name: Forgemaster Grad
pages: In this foundry we forge a blade by a hundred small corrections, not one mighty strike. Boosting works the same way. Forge a weak little tree; look closely at where it still fails; then forge the next tree specifically to patch those failures. Add it to the pile and repeat. The final blade is the SUM of every correction.

Note how this differs from the forest next door: their trees train independently and in parallel, ours train in sequence, each one shaped by the mistakes before it. A learning rate keeps every correction humble. Boosted trees such as XGBoost and LightGBM win more tabular tournaments than any other family. Handle with care: enough rounds can overfit.

mini.name: Ember Whelp
mini.question: What is the key difference between boosting and bagging?
options: Boosting trains models sequentially to fix prior errors; bagging trains them independently in parallel
Boosting only works on images; bagging only works on text
Boosting uses one deep tree; bagging uses no trees at all

definition: Cross-validation rotates which slice of the data is held out for testing, averaging the scores for a reliable estimate. Metrics like precision and recall describe different kinds of being right.
question: A disease test flags 10 patients; 9 truly have the disease, but it missed 6 other sick patients. Its precision is 90 percent. What is its recall?
options: 60 percent, because it found 9 of the 15 who were truly sick
90 percent, the same as its precision
40 percent, because it missed 6 patients out of 10 flagged
vocab: F1 score: the harmonic mean of precision and recall, useful when you need one number that punishes neglecting either.

tutor.name: Judge Kfold
pages: One test on one held-out slice can mislead: perhaps you got a lucky slice. So the Proving Grounds run k trials. Cut the data into k folds. Each round, one fold judges while the rest train. Average the k scores and you have a far steadier verdict, with every example judging exactly once.

And beware the single number. Accuracy flatters lazy models when classes are lopsided: predicting 'no dragon' is 99 percent accurate if dragons are rare. Ask two sharper questions instead. Precision: of all the times you cried dragon, how often was it real? Recall: of all real dragons, how many did you catch? Choose the metric that matches the cost of each mistake.

mini.name: Foldling
mini.question: Why can accuracy be misleading on heavily imbalanced classes?
options: Always predicting the majority class scores high while learning nothing
Accuracy cannot be computed when classes are imbalanced
Accuracy only works for regression problems

definition: K-means groups unlabeled data into k clusters by repeating two steps: assign each point to its nearest centroid, then move each centroid to the mean of its assigned points, until nothing changes.
question: What are the two alternating steps of the k-means algorithm?
options: Assign points to the nearest centroid, then move each centroid to its points' mean
Sort the points, then split the sorted list into k equal parts
Label the points by hand, then train a classifier on the labels
vocab: Centroid: the center of a cluster, computed as the average position of every point currently assigned to it.

tutor.name: Miner Centroid
pages: Down here there are no labels, traveler. Nobody tells us which gems belong together; we must let the stones sort themselves. K-means starts by dropping k torches at random spots. Every gem walks to its nearest torch. Then each torch moves to the center of the gems that chose it. Gems reconsider, torches move again, around and around until all is still.

Two warnings from an old miner. First, YOU must choose k, and the caves will not tell you; the elbow method plots how tightness improves as k grows and looks for the bend. Second, a bad starting drop can trap the torches in a poor arrangement, so run it several times and keep the best. K-means loves round, blob-shaped clusters and struggles with long snaking veins.

mini.name: Centroid Crab
mini.question: What makes clustering different from classification?
options: Clustering finds structure in unlabeled data; classification learns from labeled examples
Clustering is always more accurate than classification
Clustering only works with exactly two groups

definition: Agglomerative hierarchical clustering starts with every point as its own cluster and repeatedly merges the two closest clusters, building a tree of merges called a dendrogram that you can cut at any height.
question: What does cutting a dendrogram at a chosen height give you?
options: A particular number of clusters, without re-running the algorithm
The single best value of k, chosen automatically
A ranking of features by importance
vocab: Linkage: the rule for measuring distance between clusters, such as nearest members (single), farthest members (complete), or average.

tutor.name: Weaver Linkage
pages: Look up at the great root system on the ceiling. Every rootlet begins alone, then joins its nearest neighbor, and those strands join others, weaving upward until one trunk holds everything. Agglomerative clustering grows the same way: start with each point as its own cluster, and at every step merge the two closest clusters.

The weaving records its whole history in a dendrogram. Slice it near the bottom for many small clusters; slice near the trunk for a few large ones. No need to choose k in advance, and the tree itself often reveals how many natural groups exist. Your choice of linkage changes the weave: single linkage chains, complete linkage keeps clusters compact.

mini.name: Linkage Leech
mini.question: How does agglomerative hierarchical clustering begin?
options: Every point starts as its own tiny cluster
All points start together in one giant cluster
It starts from k random centroids

definition: PCA reduces dimensionality by finding new perpendicular axes, the principal components, ordered by how much variance of the data each captures. Keeping only the first few compresses the data while losing little information.
question: What is the first principal component of a dataset?
options: The direction along which the data varies the most
The feature column with the largest average value
The first column that appears in the data file
vocab: Explained variance ratio: the share of the data's total variance that a component captures. It tells you how many components are worth keeping.

tutor.name: Archivist Component
pages: This vault once stored scrolls with a thousand numbers each, and we were drowning. Then we noticed most of those numbers moved together. PCA finds the hidden axes of a dataset: the first component is the single direction along which the data spreads the most, the second is the most-spreading direction perpendicular to the first, and so on.

Project your scrolls onto the first few components and you keep most of the story in a fraction of the space. We use it to compress features, to silence noisy dimensions, and to draw thousand-dimensional data on a flat map with two axes. One ritual first: center and scale your features, or the loudest unit shouts over the true shape.

mini.name: Axis Moth
mini.question: What is a common use of PCA?
options: Reducing many correlated features to a few informative dimensions
Assigning class labels to unlabeled data points
Increasing the number of features in a dataset

definition: Anomaly detection learns what normal data looks like and flags points that deviate strongly from it. It shines when anomalies are too rare, too costly, or too novel to collect as labeled examples.
question: Why is anomaly detection often used instead of a plain classifier for fraud?
options: Fraud examples are rare and ever-changing, so it is easier to model normal behavior and flag deviations
Classifiers are physically unable to output a fraud label
Anomaly detection never produces false alarms
vocab: False positive rate: how often normal cases get wrongly flagged. Set your alarm threshold with this cost in mind.

tutor.name: Sentinel Odd
pages: I guard the abyss by knowing the ordinary by heart. Ten thousand honest merchants cross this bridge for every smuggler, and the smugglers keep inventing new tricks, so collecting labeled smuggler examples is a fool's errand. Instead I study NORMAL: the usual weights, routes, and hours. Whatever falls far outside that pattern gets a lantern raised.

There are many lanterns to choose from: distance to nearest neighbors, density of the local region, isolation forests that measure how few random cuts it takes to fence a point off alone. All share one dilemma: raise the threshold and you miss smugglers, lower it and you harass merchants. Choose by the price of each mistake.

mini.name: Oddity Ooze
mini.question: An isolation forest isolates anomalies quickly because anomalous points are what?
options: Few and far from the dense mass of normal points
Always the largest values in the dataset
Grouped tightly together in one big cluster

definition: Recommender systems predict what a user will like. Collaborative filtering uses the tastes of similar users or items, while content-based filtering matches item attributes to what the user liked before.
question: 'Adventurers who bought this rope also bought this lantern' is an example of what?
options: Collaborative filtering, based on patterns across many users' behavior
Content-based filtering, based on the physical attributes of rope
Anomaly detection, because buying a lantern is unusual
vocab: Cold start: the struggle to recommend for a brand-new user or item that has no interaction history yet.

tutor.name: Merchant Echo
pages: How do I know you will love this map, stranger? Two schools of divination. Content-based: I look at WHAT you liked before, note its attributes, dungeon maps, waterproof, and offer more of the same. Collaborative: I ignore attributes entirely and listen to the echoes of shoppers whose past purchases mirror yours, then offer what THEY loved that you have not tried.

Collaborative filtering can surprise you with things unlike anything you have bought, because it rides the taste of your hidden twins. Its weakness is the cold start: a new customer has no echoes yet, and a new item has no buyers. Real markets blend both schools, and matrix factorization compresses the giant user-by-item ledger into hidden taste dimensions.

mini.name: Echo Eel
mini.question: What is the cold start problem?
options: New users or items lack the history needed to make good recommendations
Recommenders only work in warm climates
Model training cannot begin until servers warm up

definition: An artificial neuron multiplies each input by a weight, sums them with a bias, and passes the result through a nonlinear activation function. Without nonlinearity, stacked layers would collapse into one linear map.
question: Why do neural networks need nonlinear activation functions?
options: Without them, any stack of layers collapses into a single linear transformation
They make the network's file size smaller on disk
They guarantee the network can never overfit
vocab: ReLU: the activation max(0, x). Brutally simple, cheap, and the default workhorse of deep networks.

tutor.name: Sparkkeeper Wattson
pages: Peer into the trench and watch a single spark think. It gathers signals from its neighbors, but it does not treat them equally: each connection has a WEIGHT, learned over time. The spark multiplies, sums, adds its own bias, and then faces the gate: the activation function decides how strongly it fires onward.

That gate must bend. If every spark passed signals along straight, a thousand layers would do no more than one, since lines composed with lines stay lines. The bend, a ReLU's kink or a sigmoid's curve, is what lets stacked layers sculpt wild shapes. Layer the sparks wide and deep, and the trench can approximate nearly any function.

mini.name: Perceptron Pup
mini.question: What does a single artificial neuron compute?
options: A weighted sum of inputs plus a bias, passed through an activation
The product of all inputs with no weights involved
The median value of its inputs

definition: Training a network means minimizing a loss function. Backpropagation applies the chain rule to compute how the loss changes with each weight, and gradient descent nudges every weight a small step downhill.
question: What does backpropagation actually compute?
options: The gradient of the loss with respect to every weight in the network
The best learning rate to use for the dataset
The number of layers the network should have
vocab: Learning rate: the size of each downhill step. Too large overshoots the valley, too small crawls forever.

tutor.name: Deepsmith Delve
pages: The chasm is a landscape of LOSS: every possible setting of the network's weights is a place to stand, and the altitude is how wrong the network is there. Training is mountaineering in reverse: from wherever you stand, feel the slope beneath your boots and step downhill. That slope is the gradient, and the step size is the learning rate.

But how do you feel the slope of a million weights at once? Backpropagation. Run an example forward, measure the error at the mouth of the chasm, then pass blame backward layer by layer with the chain rule, so every weight learns exactly how it contributed to the miss. Repeat over batches, and the network walks itself down into the valley.

mini.name: Gradient Ghast
mini.question: What happens if the learning rate is set far too high?
options: Steps overshoot the minimum and the loss can bounce or explode
Training becomes perfectly safe but slow
The network's weights stop changing entirely

definition: A convolutional neural network slides small learned filters across an image, so the same pattern detector works at every location. Early layers find edges and textures; deeper layers combine them into shapes and objects.
question: Why do CNNs slide the same small filter across the whole image?
options: A useful pattern like an edge should be detected no matter where it appears, and sharing weights makes this cheap
Because images are stored as one long row of pixels
To ensure every pixel gets its own private set of weights
vocab: Pooling: shrinking a feature map by keeping a summary such as the max of each small region, adding tolerance to small shifts.

tutor.name: Tidecaller Kernel
pages: Watch the reef fish patrol. Each one knows a single pattern, a stripe, a spiral, a glint, and sweeps the entire reef looking for it. A convolutional layer works the same way: a small filter of weights slides across the image, lighting up wherever its pattern appears. One detector, reused everywhere, because a fin is a fin in any corner of the sea.

Stack the layers and the magic compounds: the first school of filters finds edges, the next combines edges into curves and textures, deeper schools assemble eyes, fins, whole creatures. Between layers, pooling shrinks the maps and forgives small shifts. Weight sharing keeps the whole reef astonishingly light compared to connecting every pixel to everything.

mini.name: Filter Fry
mini.question: In a trained CNN, what do the earliest layers typically detect?
options: Simple patterns such as edges and color blobs
Whole objects such as faces or ships
The final class label directly

definition: Recurrent networks process sequences one step at a time while carrying a hidden state, a running memory of what came before. Gated variants like LSTMs protect that memory so it can survive long sequences.
question: What allows a recurrent network to use earlier parts of a sequence when reading later parts?
options: A hidden state passed from each step to the next as a running memory
Reading the entire sequence backwards first
Storing the whole dataset inside each neuron
vocab: Vanishing gradient: over many time steps the training signal can shrink toward zero, making long-range memories hard to learn. Gates in LSTMs fight this.

tutor.name: Currentkeeper Eddy
pages: Words, notes, tides: some data only makes sense in ORDER. A recurrent network reads a sequence the way you read a story, one token at a time, while carrying a satchel called the hidden state. At each step it blends what it just read with what the satchel holds, then passes the satchel onward. By the last word, the satchel remembers the journey.

But a long journey wears a plain satchel thin: gradients passed back through many steps can vanish, so early context fades. LSTMs and GRUs add gates, little keepers that decide what to store, what to discard, and what to reveal, letting memories survive hundreds of steps. Sequence models power translation, speech, and forecasting, though a newer throne now looms below.

mini.name: Loop Lamprey
mini.question: Which kind of data most needs a sequence model?
options: Data where order matters, such as sentences or time series
A table of independent rows in random order
A single still photograph

definition: Transformers process all positions of a sequence in parallel and use self-attention, where every token computes how relevant every other token is to it and gathers information accordingly. They are the foundation of modern large language models.
question: What does self-attention let each token in a sequence do?
options: Look at every other token and weigh how relevant each one is to itself
See only the single token immediately before it
Delete tokens that appear too frequently
vocab: Query, key, value: each token asks with its query, is indexed by its key, and shares its value. Attention scores are query-key matches used to blend values.

tutor.name: Throne Sage Vekta
pages: You stand at the deepest throne, where every word holds court at once. In the sentence 'the shard glowed because it was charged', what is 'it'? A recurrent satchel might have blurred the answer over distance. Attention simply lets 'it' gaze across the whole court and ask every word: how relevant are you to me? The shard answers loudest, and 'it' draws meaning directly from it.

Every token does this simultaneously, asking with queries, answering to keys, sharing values, no step-by-step march required. Stack attention with feedforward layers and you have the Transformer: parallel, hungry for data, and scalable beyond anything before it. This architecture is the foundation of modern language models, and of the very guide who has narrated your quest. The court is adjourned. Go claim your crown.

mini.name: Query Quill
mini.question: Compared to RNNs, how do Transformers read a sequence?
options: All positions at once in parallel, using attention instead of recurrence
One token at a time, strictly left to right, always
Only the first and last tokens of the sequence
